title: Blank file with wrong uid/gid on host with rootless layered mount
body:
<!--
---------------------------------------------------
BUG REPORT INFORMATION
---------------------------------------------------
Use the commands below to provide key information from your environment:
You do NOT have to include this information if this is a FEATURE REQUEST

**NOTE** A large number of issues reported against Podman are often found to already be fixed
in more current versions of the project.  Before reporting an issue, please verify the
version you are running with `podman version` and compare it to the latest release
documented on the top of Podman's [README.md](../README.md).  If they differ, please
update your version of Podman to the latest possible and retry your command before creating
an issue.

Also, there is a running list of known issues in the [Podman Troubleshooting Guide](https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/main/troubleshooting.md),
please reference that page before opening a new issue.

If you are filing a bug against `podman build`, please instead file a bug
against Buildah (https://github.com/containers/buildah/issues). Podman build
executes Buildah to perform container builds, and as such the Buildah
maintainers are best equipped to handle these bugs.
-->

**Is this a BUG REPORT or FEATURE REQUEST? (leave only one on its own line)**

/kind bug

**Description**

<!--
Briefly describe the problem you are having in a few paragraphs.
-->

I am currently using `--userns=auto:gidmapping:..,uidmapping:...` to bind mount paths to my rootless user account. This works exceptionally well thus far.

Here is an example, where the image user is set to `nginx`:

```
podman run --rm -it --userns=auto:gidmapping=101:0:1,uidmapping=101:0:1 --volume ./templates:/etc/nginx/templates docker.io/nginxinc/nginx-unprivileged:alpine
```

However, in some rare case where I want to add an additional file to the same mount:

```
podman run --rm -it --userns=auto:gidmapping=101:0:1,uidmapping=101:0:1 --volume ./templates:/etc/nginx/templates --volume ./test.conf.template:/etc/nginx/templates/test.conf.template docker.io/nginxinc/nginx-unprivileged:alpine
```

The top mount source `./templates` ends up with a new blank file `./templates/test.conf.template` with the wrong uid/gid outside the container. I'm wondering if I'm just missing an option to prevent that file from being created on the host so that it is only visible in the container.

The issue doesn't appear in large when running without the `--userns` option and using the root image user, as the user and group for the bind mounts are set to root inside the container and map correctly to the current rootless user, but the blank file is still created.

**Steps to reproduce the issue:**

1. Run a rootless container with `--userns=auto:gidmapping=...,uidmapping=...` and map uig/gid to that of the image.

2. Create two bind mount volumes, one mounting a directory and second one mounting a file from the host located elsewhere inside the same container destination path

3. See that a blank file now exists inside the first bind source directory with the wrong uid/gid on the host.

**Describe the results you received:**

```
ls -lah templates/
drwxr-xr-x user      user        8 B Wed Aug 31 00:09:23 2022  .
drwxr-xr-x user      user      140 B Wed Aug 31 00:09:19 2022  ..
.rwx------ 103069 103069   0 B Wed Aug 31 00:09:23 2022  test.conf.template
```

**Describe the results you expected:**

```
ls -lah templates/
drwxr-xr-x user      user        8 B Wed Aug 31 00:09:23 2022  .
drwxr-xr-x user      user      140 B Wed Aug 31 00:09:19 2022  ..
```

**Additional information you deem important (e.g. issue happens only occasionally):**

**Output of `podman version`:**

```
Client:       Podman Engine
Version:      4.2.0
API Version:  4.2.0
Go Version:   go1.19
Git Commit:   7fe5a419cfd2880df2028ad3d7fd9378a88a04f4-dirty
Built:        Sat Aug 13 03:26:55 2022
OS/Arch:      linux/amd64
```

**Output of `podman info`:**

```

```

comments.body:
Before you can use it with the second command, the `test.conf.template` directory must exist, how was it created?

If you'd like to force the user ownership, you can probably use the `:U` option to the mount:

```
podman run --rm -it --userns=auto:gidmapping=101:0:1,uidmapping=101:0:1 --volume ./templates:/etc/nginx/templates --volume:U ./test.conf.template:/etc/nginx/templates/test.conf.template:U docker.io/nginxinc/nginx-unprivileged:alpine
```

Does it solve the issue for you?

@giuseppe `test.conf.template` is a file and already exists.

I think what is happening here is that it is correctly creating a layered bind mount of sorts, but doing so creates a residual blank file in the directory where it is mounted on the host (presumably with the wrong permissions as well).

1st bind mount:
```
./templates/:/etc/nginx/templates/
```

2nd bind mount:
```
./test.conf.template:/etc/nginx/templates/test.conf.template
```

> Note, that the 2nd bind mount is inside the 1st

There may be special flags that address this situation, but I haven't found them. What is expected here is for it to work like an overlay volume mount, but not read only and without the residual file. You can emulate this with:

```
podman run --rm -it --userns=auto:gidmapping=101:0:1,uidmapping=101:0:1 --volume ./templates:/etc/nginx/templates:O --volume ./test.conf.template:/etc/nginx/templates/test.conf.template docker.io/nginxinc/nginx-unprivileged:alpine
```

This correctly mounts and does not create the residual file, but note you get an error if you mount to a named volume (`VOLUME /etc/nginx/templates`) from the image.

> Likely a bug in the implementation. It appears to work with a named volume when using a trailing slash, `./templates:/etc/nginx/templates/:O`, but then the existing files in `etc/nginx/templates` end up missing.

It also seems to quickly get out of sync. Although merely echoing changes to `test.conf.template` reflect on the host and in the container, after opening and editing in Vim on the host it breaks the sync for some reason. The overlay option may be the correct route and addressing any issues within that implementation, but I also think it is safe to say that layered bind mounts should be expected to work like the overlay as well as it defeats the entire purpose otherwise.

with `:O` the changes are not reflected in the volume since there is an overlay mount happening on top of the first bind mount, so all the changes you perform there are made on the overlay upper directory.  So the `test.conf.template` inode is created on the upper mount.

In the other case instead there is no overlay mount and the changes are reflected directly in the first mount directory.  I don't think we can do anything about it, the kernel needs the inode to create the bind mount.
I think we can close this issue and continue the conversation here.
